Adventure #6—The Write Stuff
The sound coming from my new neighbor’s garage at eleven o’clock at night was making it harder and harder
Adventure #7—Oh, What a Tangled for me to concentrate on the spreadsheets staring back at me from my glaring laptop screen.	Web We Weave Previous Module Next Lesson	Mark as Complete
Adventure #8—To Sleep, Perchance
to Come Up with Great Ideas “Thump… thumpity… thump .”
Adventure #9—The Beginning
Postscript... Four Years Later The distraction was just as well, for the spreadsheets didn’t paint a very pretty picture. Downright depressing,
actually. Even more depressing was the terse text message I’d received earlier from my store’s landlord. I
looked at my phone and read it again:
Still haven’t received rent payment.
Now several months owed.
Will you be continuing in this location?
Please advise.
Most depressing of all, however, was what I had had to tell my oldest daughter just a few minutes before. She
had just been accepted to one of the top private colleges in the country—the one she had her heart set on
attending since junior high.
But being a single dad and trying to keep a failing business afloat had put quite a strain on our finances, and I
had been forced to tell her that it looked like she would have to attend a less expensive public college. She’d
tried so hard not to let it show, but her disappointment was clear. I felt awful.
One thing was for sure—I needed ideas for turning around my business, and I needed them fast.
“Thump… thumpity… thump .”
Ideas. Yes, I needed ideas. But where would they come from? I had already tried everything I knew. I needed new
ideas— new ways of thinking and of looking at things. I had known that for some time now, yet I kept coming up
blank. “Think outside the box,” I reminded myself.
But inside or outside, the cupboard seemed bare.
My inability to come up with ideas was making me more and more pessimistic. And my pessimism seemed to
be preventing me from coming up with any ideas. It was a vicious circle it seemed impossible to get out of.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 9 --

Maybe if I just—
“Thump… thumpity… THUMP! ”
That did it! It was time to introduce myself to my new neighbor and inform him that the rules of good
neighborship did not include making a racket at a time when most good people—whose businesses weren’t
down the tubes—are getting ready for bed.
***
He opened the side door of his garage with an outstretched hand and an apologetic grin that made it difficult for
me to keep my anger stoked to full boil. “I’m so sorry,” he began. “Was all that noise keeping you up? I’m Tom.”
I shook his hand, about to introduce myself, but he didn’t give me a moment. “I had an idea, you see, and when I
get an idea in my head, I just have to put together a rough model to see if it’ll work—no matter the time of day.”
I looked around his garage. Although it could have held two cars, there were none in sight. Instead, all available
space was taken up by a hodgepodge of tables and shelves jam-packed with mechanisms of all shapes and
sizes. Each one seemed in a different stage of being assembled or taken apart—it was hard to tell which.
“Well, I live next door,” I announced. “And it’s impossible for me to—”
“Sorry,” Tom interrupted, holding his hand up, “I’m going to have to ask you not to use that word in my workshop.”
“What word?” I asked, wondering what kind of a crank I had for a neighbor.
“The ‘i’ word that means something isn’t possible. I learned a long time ago that using words like that and talking
about what can’t be done only eliminates possibilities that might hold valuable solutions. In this room, there is
only what is possible.”
“I see,” I said, although I didn’t really. Then, changing the subject, I added, “I heard you’re an inventor. Are you…
inventing something?”
Tom laughed good-naturedly. “Well, I suppose you could call me an inventor. But unfortunately, most people think
of an inventor as an inveterate tinkerer who’s a few wires short of a complete circuit, if you know what I mean… a
mad scientist fixated on some idea he thinks will change the world, but that will more than likely go up in a puff of
smoke—both literally as well as figuratively.
So instead, I prefer to think of myself as…” (and here he seemed to pause somewhat dramatically) “a ‘problem
solver.’”
“I see. And what types of problems do you solve?”
“Well, take this one here, for example,” he said, picking up a metal tank the size of a large suitcase. It had a
large hole cut out of the top, a drainplug at the base, and what seemed to be a small microphone with a wire
coming out of it attached to the bottom.
“A company I work with has a factory that uses an extremely corrosive acid in their manufacturing process. They
need to know when the tank that supplies this acid is empty. They’ve tried fitting the tank with every kind of
measuring device known to man, but the acid and its fumes make all of them inoperable in a short time. So, they
brought the problem to me.”
“What did you do?”
“Well, the first thing I did was define the problem.”
“Define the problem? Isn’t that obvious?”
“No, it’s not obvious at all. The problem with the way most people go about solving problems is that they aren’t
clear what the problem really is that they’re trying to solve. For example, the people at the factory thought the
problem was designing a device that could measure the amount of acid left in the tank and withstand the highly
corrosive environment.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 9 --

“So, they kept failing. But in talking to them, I was able to define the problem slightly differently. I asked: How can
the tank tell us when it’s empty? And that’s a much easier problem to solve.”
“Sure,” I said, tongue in cheek. “All you need to do is design a talking tank.”
“Yes, exactly ,” he continued, unfazed by my sarcasm, “in which case, we don’t need measuring devices that are
vulnerable to the highly acidic environment. All we need are some hollow, acid-resistant steel balls with a lower
specific gravity than that of the acid.”
“So… they would float?” I ventured.
“Yes, exactly, so they would float— until the tank runs out of acid. Then, they’re designed to roll around the bottom
of the tank and make what would technically be called a ‘racket.’ And that is how the tank ‘tells’ us that it’s
empty.”
He then pulled out the stopper on his model tank. The water quickly drained out into a pan and the little balls
began rolling around on the bottom. The sound was quickly picked up by the microphone and amplified over a
nearby speaker, making the ‘thumpity… thump… thump’ sound that had gotten my attention earlier.
“As you can see—I mean, as you can hear —I’ve also used a mic and speaker to capture, amplify and convey
the sound. This way, you don’t need someone sitting by the tank all day listening for the balls to begin rattling
around. The sound could be conveyed to any office or to the factory’s control center. If you wanted to get a little
more sophisticated, it would be simple enough to rig the sound to trigger another device, such as an alert on the
foreman’s cell.”
“Wow,” I said, rocking on my feet. “How do you come up with ideas like that?”
“It’s simple,” he replied. “You just define the problem correctly. Often that gives you the answer right there. If not, I
very systematically go about finding the solution.”
“A system, huh? I always thought problem solving was un systematic—free form, outside the box, brainstorm-y or
whatever.”
“Well, I think that’s also part of the problem with the way most people go about solving problems. It’s very hit or
miss. And by trying to think ‘outside the box,’ all you do is give yourself such a vast area to look in that you’re
overwhelmed right from the get-go.
“Instead, I prefer to think inside the box—15 specific, pre-defined boxes I’ve come up with, to be exact.
‘Thought starters,’ you might call them, that get you looking for solutions in ways and in places you might not
otherwise think to look.
“To solve this particular problem,” he said, holding up the tank, “I looked in the first box—the one I call Add a New
Dimension . And in engineering, one of the ways you can add a new dimension is by adding something you can
see, hear, feel or smell.
“I could have used an optical system. For example, I might have tried to dye the acid and make a side of the tank
transparent, to see when the acid had run out. However, I wasn’t sure acid would hold coloring, and I wasn’t sure
the acid wouldn’t dissolve any transparent tints I could create. That’s why I went with something you could hear—
the hollow steel balls that make a rattling sound when the acid runs out.”
“That’s pretty neat,” I replied. “I can see where that kind of ‘inside-the-box’ thinking would work for mechanical
stuff. But can it also be used for non-mechanical problems?”
“Absolutely,” Tom replied. “When I first learned about this system, I thought of it only in terms of engineering-type
problems. But the more I worked with it, the more I saw that it could be used to solve other types of problems, as
well. Everyday problems. Personal problems. Business problems. You name it.”
“Fascinating. You know, I’ve got a business problem or two,” I said, vastly understating the situation. “Would you
be open to showing me how I might use your process on my business?”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 9 --

“Sure,” said Tom. “You own a bookstore, right?”
“As of right now I do,” I answered. “Not sure for how much longer, though.” I took my phone from my pocket and
showed him the message from my landlord.
Tom read it, nodding. “Well then, it looks like we’ve got no time to waste to get you thinking in some new ways.
OK, let’s start from the top. How do you think you could apply the box of Add a New Dimension to your
problem?”
I could feel my shoulders tensing up as they always did when under pressure to come up with an answer quickly
or be creative.
“Relax,” said Tom, picking up on my anxiety. “The stress of coming up with new ideas comes from having a big,
vast area you have to look through.
“We’re just going to look in one tiny little box, and we’ll take it one step at a time. As I already told you, the first
part of this box is adding the dimension of either sound, feeling, sight or smell. Let’s take them one at a time,
applying them to a specific problem that you have. What should we try it on first?”
“Well,” I said, choosing the first and most obvious problem that came to mind, “I’d like to get more customers
into my store.”
“Great! Now, how might you get more people into your store using visual, auditory, kinesthetic—which is
feeling—or olfactory elements?”
Now this was something I could wrap my mind around. “Let me think… something you can see. Maybe I could
get more people to come in if I had a TV in the sitting area where the customer (or their significant other who’s
less partial to reading) could watch Netflix, sports or the news?
“Something you could hear…” I continued. “I could get live music! People love live entertainment—it would bring
them in as they walk by, and I could advertise it as an event. It would be so easy to get students or others to
play—it’s good practice, fun and gives them great publicity.”
“Something you can feel,” prompted Tom, nodding along.
“Let’s see… I could light a fire in the fireplace on colder days. Let the kids toast marshmallows. Offer hot cider!”
“Smell,” Tom pushed.
“That’s easy. I’ve noticed at movie theaters that the smell of popcorn really carries. I bet if I popped some
popcorn, the aroma would waft out to the street and draw people in. And, I could advertise that we provide free
popcorn to anyone who enters the store. People love free stuff. And another thing I could do is—”
“Okay, okay,” Tom interrupted. “I think you get the idea! And that’s just the first part of this box. You can also
explore other dimensions by adding to or replacing things. You can work with the dimension of movement by
making an object or process that moves stationary—or making one that’s stationary move. You can make
something change over time or stop something from changing. And you can go from random to ordered, or from
ordered to random.”
“Stationary to moving,” I repeated slowly. “That doesn’t really apply to bookselling.”
Tom raised his eyebrows, challenging me.
“Although now that I think about it, books shelved in a bookstore are very stationary, which can be limiting. Why
couldn’t I find a way to take books to people at different events—like a little kiosk selling sports books at
sporting events? Business books where business groups and associations have meetings. That could open up
whole new markets for us! What was next? Turn something unchanging into something that changes?”
Tom smiled approvingly.
“The menu in our café hasn’t changed since we opened it. Why couldn’t there be at least one or two new weekly
specials? People might come in just to see what we have.”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 9 --

“Random to ordered?” Tom offered.
I thought for a moment. “It’s random the way people come into the store, and whatever salesperson happens to
be available serves them. What if this process were more ordered, say, by assigning a specific salesperson to
each of our best customers? That way, the customer could call anytime, and their personal salesperson—or a
book concierge —could keep them informed of when new books of interest are coming in.”
“You’re really getting the hang of this,” said Tom. “Now, when you look inside this box in the future, don’t just limit
yourself to the dimensions we’ve discussed: the senses, movement, change and so on. Feel free to add your
own and look at or emphasize a new dimension of any object, part or process.”
I felt like a long-suffering prospector who had finally found an infallible gold detector that could spot gold
wherever it was hidden. “I don’t know when I’ve been able to come up with so many new ideas! For goodness
sake,” I exclaimed, “ what are all the other boxes? ”
“Don’t worry,” said my new friend. “It’s late and they’ll still be here tomorrow. Why don’t you stop by in the
evening, and then we can continue our little discussion?”
“You can count on it.”
“Oh, and by the way… come hungry,” he winked.
Summary & Exercises
In a Nutshell
Adventure #1 introduced you to this revolutionary Idea Power system and revealed the three fundamentals of
creativity and problem solving:
1. Don’t use the word “impossible” — This is just a way of saying that when you’re trying to be creative or
solve a problem, it really, really helps to have a “can-do” attitude.
That’s because: 1) The so-called “impossible” may very well prove to be possible—literally or in some slightly
different way, and 2) Considering an “impossible” alternative can sometimes shed light on the situation in a way
that leads you to a very possible answer.
2. Defin e the problem or what you’re trying to accomplish first. In medicine, they say that the cure is only
as good as the diagnosis. Likewise, the quality of your solution depends on how well you have defined your
problem.
Precisely defining your challenge or opportunity helps you really understand what you’re trying to accomplish.
Otherwise, you waste time coming up with ideas that don’t really apply. Just as the sun’s rays when focused by a
magnifying glass will burn through paper, focus can enable you to burn through obstacles to deeper levels of
creativity.
Clearer problem definition can also make obstacles disappear.
Take the classic creativity problem: nine dots arranged in a square that you’re supposed to connect by using
only four straight lines.
Many people find this problem unsolvable because they define the problem as “How can I connect the nine dots
using four straight lines within the square formed by the outer dots?”
However, the problem is easily solved if you redefine it as: “How can I connect the nine dots by using four
straight lines whether I go outside the border or not? ” (See below for the solution at the end of this section.)
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 9 --

Another example…
When NASA first began sending men into space, they had to deal with the tremendous heat (3,500 degrees!)
generated upon re-entry. At first, they defined the problem as “What substance can withstand such intense
heat?” Unfortunately, the answer was, “None that we know of.”
Finally, someone redefined the problem as “How can we keep the spaceship cool?” This line of inquiry led to a
shield that, in fact, couldn’t withstand the heat of re-entry. But it burned off in layers, dissipating the heat and
protecting the vehicle.
If a store owner wants to make more money, he could define the problem as: “How do I get more people to visit
my shop?” Or “How do I get more people who visit my shop to buy?” Or “How do I get each customer to buy
more?” Or “How can I cut my expenses to make more profit?
Each definition has very different solutions and very different business implications.
In relationships, how each person defines problems is critical to their successful resolution. Each person has
three choices. For example: “The problem is how I act” versus “The problem is how the other person acts”
versus “The problem is how we interact.
Each definition takes them down a completely different path.
Another way to think about this is in terms of levels of abstraction.
The best innovation takes place at a higher level of abstraction.
When you ask: “How can I create a better X?”, that’s a low level of abstraction.
And you may get a better X.
But if you ask: “How can I create something that does Y better?”, that’s a higher level of abstraction.
Because then, instead of improving X, you may come up with a totally different and better way of accomplishing
what X accomplishes.
In other words, if you ask: “How can I make a better taxi company?”, you may create a slightly better taxi
company.
But if you ask, “What is a better way to get people from one place to another?”, you may create a whole new
mode of transportation, such as Uber.
So let’s take a little time right now to identify a specific problem that you have at home or at the office. This will
be the problem that you will be working on throughout Idea Power.
(DO NOT skip this exercise —
It’s the foundation of everything that follows…)
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 9 --

Click here to download this fillable PDF View Full Screen
3. The third fundamental principle that you learned in Adventure #1 is the idea of thinking inside the
box instead of out of it— of narrowing your search in order to make it more fruitful. This, too, focuses your
efforts in a way that concentrates and multiplies your idea-generating power.
Which leads us to…
The Idea-Generating Boxes of This System
Before we start working with the 15 specific boxes, it’s very important for you to understand that the sole function
of these idea-starter boxes is to help you.
This means there is no “right” or “wrong” way to use them. So relax and have fun. Remember that whatever they
inspire you to come up with is absolutely, 100% correct.
It may help to take each question in a box in two ways.
First, literally. For example, if the question suggests coming up with ideas that have to do with speed, think of
things that literally have to do with speed—such as doing something faster or slower.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 9 --

But then, let yourself go and think of things that may only indirectly have to do with speed—or even things you just
happen to associate with speed. The important thing is to come up with good ideas—and not worry about
getting the “right” answer to the question.
Of course, you don’t have to answer each and every question in the boxes provided (although it’s good practice
to at least try ).
Now…
Working with the specific problem you identified and defined above, let’s start with Box #1: “Add a New
Dimension.”
Ready? Let’s play…
Click here to download this fillable PDF View Full Screen
Your Action Steps for Adventure #1
1. Try to observe your attitude about problem solving and coming up with ideas. Are you generally optimistic?
Pessimistic? Somewhere in between? Try to be more optimistic and see if it doesn’t improve your creativity.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 9 --

2. Problems and opportunities have many dimensions to them. But we tend to have a narrow focus and only see
a few. Practice seeing with a wider vision. Find the many different aspects or dimensions of the situation you’re
exploring or the goal you want to accomplish. This will give you so many more ways to approach them.
MODULES Next, if you haven’t yet, please read or watch Adventure #2 to discover how the Slicing and Dicing boxes get you
thinking in new and profitable ways.
A problem well defined is well on its way to being solved.
All of these resources are also available in your Idea Power Mindbook if you prefer to write
them out by hand.
And here’s the solution to that puzzle…
